perturbation.original_text: All payments hereunder will be in US Dollars and are due and payable within thirty (30) days after the date of invoice.
perturbation.changed_text: All payments hereunder will be in US Dollars and are due and payable within sixty (60) days after the date of invoice.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment term from 30 to 60 days could violate California Prompt Payment laws if the payments are related to construction or other industries with shorter payment deadlines.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 7159.5 requires that home improvement contracts specify the payment schedule, which must comply with specific limits regarding down payments and progress payments.  Extending the payment terms to 60 days, particularly if the agreement involves services covered by this section, may violate these regulations, as it could delay payments beyond legally allowable timeframes depending on the specific structure of the payment schedule and nature of the work.

PROFESSIONS AND VOCATIONS GENERALLY [5000 - 9998.11] ( Heading of Division 3 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 30. ) CHAPTER 9. Contractors [7000 - 7191] ( Chapter 9 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) ARTICLE 10. Home Improvement Business [7150 - 7170] ( Article 10 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 1021. ) 7159.5. This section applies to all home improvement contracts, as defined in Section 7151.2, between an owner or tenant and a contractor, whether a general contractor or a specialty contractor, that is licensed or subject to be licensed pursuant to this chapter with regard to the transaction. (a) Failure by the licensee or a person subject to be licensed under this chapter, or by their agent or salesperson, to comply with the following provisions is cause for discipline: (1) The contract shall be in writing and shall include the agreed contract amount in dollars and cents. The contract amount shall include the entire cost of the contract, including profit, labor, and materials, but excluding finance charges. (2) If there is a separate finance charge between the contractor and the person contracting for home improvement, the finance charge shall be set out separately from the contract amount. (3) If a downpayment will be charged, the d
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment term from 30 to 60 days, potentially violating California Prompt Payment laws if the payments relate to industries with shorter deadlines. The Law Explanation specifies that extending payment terms may violate regulations outlined in California Business and Professions Code Section 7159.5, particularly if the agreement involves services covered by this section. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 7159.5, highlights that this section applies to home improvement contracts and outlines requirements for payment schedules. Therefore, the changed text creates an inconsistency that could contradict California law, making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Unless an Update has been delivered to Channel Partner as a patch to correct an urgent problem (in which case distribution to End Users will be immediate upon receipt), Channel Partner will distribute to its End Users all Updates within thirty (30) days of such Update being made available to Channel Partner.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless an Update has been delivered to Channel Partner as a patch to correct an urgent problem (in which case distribution to End Users will be immediate upon receipt), Channel Partner will distribute to its End Users all Updates within ninety (90) days of such Update being made available to Channel Partner.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying security updates for 90 days could violate data security laws that require businesses to implement reasonable security measures in a timely manner. In California, failing to promptly apply necessary security updates could be seen as a failure to protect personal information adequately.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1798.81.5 mandates that businesses implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information, to protect the personal information of California residents. Delaying the distribution and implementation of software updates, especially those addressing security vulnerabilities, for an extended period of 90 days could be deemed a failure to maintain reasonable security measures, potentially violating this law if a data breach occurs as a result of the unpatched vulnerability.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.81. CUSTOMER RECORDS [1798.80 - 1798.84] ( Title 1.81 added by Stats. 2000, Ch. 1039, Sec. 1. ) 1798.81.5. (a) (1) It is the intent of the Legislature to ensure that personal information about California residents is protected. To that end, the purpose of this section is to encourage businesses that own, license, or maintain personal information about Californians to provide reasonable security for that information. (2) For the purpose of this section, the terms “own” and “license” include personal information that a business retains as part of the business’ internal customer account or for the purpose of using that information in transactions with the person to whom the information relates. The term “maintain” includes personal information that a business maintains but does not own or license. (b) A business that owns, licenses, or maintains personal information about a California resident shall implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information, to protect the personal information fro
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the timeframe for distributing updates from 30 to 90 days. The original contract mandates that channel partners distribute updates to end users within thirty days, but the modified contract extends this period to ninety days. California Civil Code Section 1798.81.5(b) states that businesses must "implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information, to protect the personal information." Delaying security updates for 90 days, rather than 30, could be viewed as a failure to maintain reasonable security, thus contradicting the law because it introduces a significant delay in implementing crucial security measures, increasing the risk of vulnerability exploitation.

perturbation.original_text: Notice will be deemed given: upon personal delivery; if sent by confirmed facsimile, upon confirmation of receipt; if sent by overnight courier, upon receipt; or, if sent by certified or registered mail, postage prepaid, three (3) days after the date of mailing.
perturbation.changed_text: Notice will be deemed given: upon personal delivery; if sent by confirmed facsimile, upon confirmation of receipt; if sent by overnight courier, upon receipt; or, if sent by certified or registered mail, postage prepaid, ten (10) days after the date of mailing.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notice period to ten days for mail delivery could conflict with laws or regulations that require faster notification, such as certain legal or financial notifications.  If this contract is used in scenarios where timely notice is legally mandated (e.g., foreclosure, eviction), a ten-day mail delivery period might be insufficient.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1162 specifies the requirements for serving notice in unlawful detainer (eviction) actions. While it doesn't explicitly state a timeframe for when notice is 'deemed given' after mailing, the overall intent of the law is to ensure prompt notice to the tenant. Setting a 'deemed given' timeframe of 10 days after mailing could be challenged if it unduly delays the proceedings and prejudices the tenant's ability to respond within the legally mandated timeframe (typically 3-5 days to respond after service).

OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. OF SUMMARY PROCEEDINGS [1132 - 1179.07] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 4. Summary Proceedings for Obtaining Possession of Real Property in Certain Cases [1159 - 1179a] ( Chapter 4 enacted 1872. ) 1162. (a) Except as provided in subdivision (b), the notices required by Sections 1161 and 1161a may be served by any of the following methods: (1) By delivering a copy to the tenant personally. (2) If he or she is absent from his or her place of residence, and from his or her usual place of business, by leaving a copy with some person of suitable age and discretion at either place, and sending a copy through the mail addressed to the tenant at his or her place of residence. (3) If such place of residence and business cannot be ascertained, or a person of suitable age or discretion there can not be found, then by affixing a copy in a conspicuous place on the property, and also delivering a copy to a person there residing, if such person can be found; and also sending a copy through the mail addressed to the tenant at the place where the property is situated. Service upon a subtenant may be made in the same manner. (b) The notices required by
perturbation.justification: The modification extends the mail delivery timeframe for notice to ten days, which could contradict the intent of California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1162. While the snippet doesn't specify the exact timeframe for when notice is 'deemed given,' the law emphasizes prompt notification in eviction actions. A ten-day period may unduly delay proceedings and negatively affect the tenant's ability to respond within the legally mandated timeframe. The changed text could be viewed as weakening the legal requirement for timely notice, potentially rendering the contractual term unenforceable, as it falls under the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction.
